name: Web Application
description: Deploy a ready-to-use web application with automatic scaling and built-in security.

steps.title: Basic Details
steps.description: Give your web application a name and choose where it will run.

fields.name: appName
fields.label: What should we call your application?
fields.helpText: This will be used as part of your application's web address.

fields.name: region
fields.label: Where should it run?
fields.helpText: Choose a location close to your users for the best performance.

steps.title: Performance Settings
steps.description: Choose how powerful your application should be.

fields.name: planSize
fields.label: How much power does your app need?
fields.helpText: You can change this later if your needs grow.

options.label: Small — Good for testing and demos
Large — High traffic or demanding apps

fields.name: autoScale
fields.label: Automatically handle traffic spikes?
fields.helpText: When enabled, your app will automatically scale up when more people use it.

steps.title: Networking
steps.description: Set up how people will access your application.

fields.name: customDomain
fields.label: Use a custom web address (domain)?
fields.helpText: Enable this if you want your app at a custom domain like myapp.company.com. You'll configure the domain separately.

fields.name: httpsOnly
fields.label: Enforce secure connections (HTTPS only)?
fields.helpText: Recommended. Ensures all traffic to your app is encrypted.

name: Virtual Machine
description: Set up a virtual computer in the cloud for running software, scripts, or development environments.

steps.title: Basic Details
steps.description: Name your virtual machine and choose its operating system.

fields.name: vmName
fields.label: What should we call this virtual machine?
fields.helpText: Use a name that describes what this machine will be used for.

fields.name: osType
fields.label: Which operating system?
fields.helpText: Choose the operating system you're most comfortable with.

fields.name: region
fields.label: Where should it run?
fields.helpText: Choose a location close to your users or your office.

steps.title: Size & Performance
steps.description: Choose how powerful your virtual machine should be.

fields.name: vmSize
fields.label: How powerful should this machine be?
fields.helpText: Larger sizes cost more but can handle heavier workloads.

options.label: Small (2 CPUs, 4 GB memory) — Light tasks
Medium (4 CPUs, 8 GB memory) — Most workloads
Large (8 CPUs, 16 GB memory) — Heavy workloads

steps.title: Access Settings
steps.description: Set up how you'll log in to this machine.

fields.name: adminUsername
fields.label: What username do you want to use to log in?
fields.helpText: You'll use this username when connecting to the machine.

fields.name: enableSSH
fields.label: Enable SSH access (for Linux machines)?
fields.helpText: Allows secure remote access to Linux machines from your computer.

name: Managed Database
description: Create a fully managed database — no server maintenance needed. Great for apps that store data.

steps.title: Basic Details
steps.description: Name your database and choose its location.

fields.name: dbName
fields.label: What should we call this database?
fields.helpText: Use a name that describes what data this database will store.

fields.name: region
fields.label: Where should it run?
fields.helpText: For best performance, choose the same location as your application.

fields.name: engineVersion
fields.label: Which database engine?
fields.helpText: PostgreSQL is the most versatile choice for most projects.

steps.title: Storage & Capacity
steps.description: Configure how much space your database needs.

fields.name: storageGB
fields.label: How much storage space (in GB)?
fields.helpText: Start with 32 GB — you can increase this later as your data grows.

fields.name: enableBackup
fields.label: Enable automatic backups?
fields.helpText: Recommended. Automatically saves a copy of your data every day so you can recover if something goes wrong.

steps.title: Security
steps.description: Control who can access your database.

fields.name: enableFirewall
fields.label: Restrict access to specific locations only?
fields.helpText: Recommended. Prevents unauthorized access by limiting who can connect to your database.

name: File Storage
description: Store files, documents, images, and data securely in the cloud — accessible from anywhere.

steps.title: Basic Details
steps.description: Name your storage and choose where it will be.

fields.name: storageName
fields.label: What should we call this storage?
fields.helpText: Use only lowercase letters and numbers. No spaces or special characters.

fields.name: region
fields.label: Where should it be stored?
fields.helpText: Choose a location close to where you'll be uploading and downloading files.

steps.title: Redundancy & Access
steps.description: Choose how your files are protected and accessed.

fields.name: redundancy
fields.label: How many copies should we keep?
fields.helpText: More copies means better protection against data loss.

options.label: 3 copies in the same location — Basic protection
6 copies across distant locations — Best protection

fields.name: accessTier
fields.label: How often will you access these files?
fields.helpText: Choose based on how often you need to access the files. Hot is faster but costs more.

steps.title: Security
steps.description: Control who can access your files.

fields.name: publicAccess
fields.label: Allow public access to files?
fields.helpText: Leave this off unless you specifically need files to be publicly accessible on the internet.

name: Private Network
description: Create an isolated private network so your cloud resources can communicate securely with each other.

steps.title: Basic Details
steps.description: Name your network and choose its location.

fields.name: vnetName
fields.label: What should we call this network?
fields.helpText: Use a name that describes what this network connects.

fields.name: region
fields.label: Where should it be created?
fields.helpText: Must be in the same location as the resources that will use this network.

steps.title: Address Space
steps.description: Define the IP address range for your network.

fields.name: addressSpace
fields.label: What IP address range should this network use?
fields.helpText: The default (10.0.0.0/16) works for most setups and allows up to 65,000 addresses.

steps.title: Subnets
steps.description: Create a subnet to organize resources within your network.

fields.name: subnetName
fields.label: What should the first subnet be called?
fields.helpText: Subnets help you organize and isolate groups of resources.

fields.name: subnetRange
fields.label: What IP range should this subnet use?
fields.helpText: Must be within your network's address range. The default (10.0.1.0/24) works for most cases.

name: Secret Manager
description: Safely store passwords, API keys, certificates, and encryption keys — away from your code.

steps.title: Basic Details
steps.description: Name your secret manager and choose its location.

fields.name: vaultName
fields.label: What should we call this secret manager?
fields.helpText: Must be globally unique. Use a name like your-project-secrets.

fields.name: region
fields.label: Where should it be created?
fields.helpText: For best performance, choose the same location as your application.

steps.title: Protection Settings
steps.description: Configure how your secrets are protected.

fields.name: softDelete
fields.label: Enable recovery period for deleted secrets?
fields.helpText: Recommended. If a secret is accidentally deleted, you can recover it within 90 days.

fields.name: purgeProtection
fields.label: Prevent permanent deletion?
fields.helpText: Prevents anyone from permanently deleting secrets until the recovery period ends. Adds extra safety.

steps.title: Access
steps.description: Choose how applications will access your secrets.

fields.name: accessModel
fields.label: How should applications access secrets?
fields.helpText: Role-based access is the modern and recommended approach.

name: Container Application
description: Run containerized applications without managing servers — just provide your container image.

steps.title: Basic Details
steps.description: Name your application and provide the container image.

fields.name: appName
fields.label: What should we call this application?
fields.helpText: This will also be part of your app's web address.

fields.name: containerImage
fields.label: What container image should we run?
fields.helpText: The Docker image name and tag, e.g. myregistry/myapp:v1.0

fields.name: region
fields.label: Where should it run?
fields.helpText: Choose a location close to your users.

steps.title: Scaling
steps.description: Configure how your application handles changing traffic.

fields.name: minReplicas
fields.label: Minimum number of running copies?
fields.helpText: Set to 0 to scale down to zero when not in use (saves cost, but adds startup delay).

fields.name: maxReplicas
fields.label: Maximum number of copies during high traffic?
fields.helpText: The app will automatically create more copies when traffic increases, up to this limit.

steps.title: Networking
steps.description: Configure external access to your application.

fields.name: externalAccess
fields.label: Allow access from the internet?
fields.helpText: Enable if you want your app accessible from outside your organization's network.

name: Landing Zone Starter
description: A complete foundation setup — includes a private network, security controls, and monitoring for your project.

steps.title: Project Details
steps.description: Name your project and choose where everything will be created.

fields.name: projectName
fields.label: What is this project called?
fields.helpText: All resources will be named based on this project name.

fields.name: region
fields.label: Where should everything be created?
fields.helpText: Choose a location close to your team or users.

steps.title: What to Include
steps.description: Choose which foundational components to set up.

fields.name: includeNetwork
fields.label: Include a private network?
fields.helpText: Recommended. Creates an isolated network so your resources can communicate securely.

fields.name: includeMonitoring
fields.label: Include monitoring and alerts?
fields.helpText: Recommended. Sets up dashboards and alerts so you know when something needs attention.

fields.name: includeSecurity
fields.label: Include security baseline?
fields.helpText: Recommended. Enables security scanning and compliance checks for your resources.

steps.title: Naming Convention
steps.description: Set a prefix to keep your resources organized.

fields.name: namingPrefix
fields.label: What prefix should all resources use?
fields.helpText: A short prefix (2-5 letters) that will be added to all resource names. Helps identify resources in billing and reports.

name: Automated Approval Workflow
description: Set up an automated approval chain — for leave requests, expense claims, or document sign-off — that runs when someone submits a form or calls a URL.

steps.title: Workflow Details
steps.description: Give your approval workflow a name and choose where it will run.

fields.name: workflowName
fields.label: What should we call this workflow?
fields.helpText: Use a name that describes what this workflow approves, e.g. 'expense-claims' or 'document-review'.

fields.name: region
fields.label: Where should it be created?
fields.helpText: Choose a location close to your users for the best performance.

steps.title: Approval Settings
steps.description: Configure how approvals are handled and how long to wait for a response.

fields.name: approvalSteps
fields.label: How many levels of approval are needed?
fields.helpText: For most use cases, 1 or 2 levels is sufficient. Add more levels for high-value or compliance-sensitive requests.

fields.name: timeoutDays
fields.label: How many days before an unanswered request is flagged?
fields.helpText: If an approver does not respond within this many days, the request will be marked as overdue. Recommended: 3 days.

name: Scheduled Task Runner
description: Run an automated task on a schedule — daily reports, weekly data syncs, or overnight batch jobs — without anyone needing to remember to do it.

steps.title: Task Details
steps.description: Give your scheduled task a name and choose where it will run.

fields.name: workflowName
fields.label: What should we call this scheduled task?
fields.helpText: Use a name that describes what this task does, e.g. 'monthly-member-report' or 'nightly-data-sync'.

fields.name: region
fields.label: Where should it run?
fields.helpText: Choose a location close to your data or systems.

steps.title: Schedule
steps.description: Set how often this task should run and at what time.

fields.name: frequency
fields.label: How often should this task run?
fields.helpText: Choose the interval that matches your business need.

fields.name: runTime
fields.label: What time should it run?
fields.helpText: All times are Malaysia time (UTC+8).

name: Reliable Message Queue
description: Create a reliable message channel between two systems so they do not need to talk to each other directly. Useful for connecting a legacy system to a new one, or handling bursts of requests without overloading a backend.

steps.title: Queue Details
steps.description: Give your message queue a name and choose where it will be created.

fields.name: namespaceName
fields.label: What should we call this message queue?
fields.helpText: Use a name that describes the systems it connects, e.g. 'hr-to-finance' or 'claims-processing'.

fields.name: region
fields.label: Where should it be created?
fields.helpText: Choose a location close to the systems that will use this queue.

steps.title: Capacity
steps.description: Choose how much load this queue needs to handle.

fields.name: tier
fields.label: How much load do you expect?
fields.helpText: Basic is free and great for testing. Standard supports larger workloads and advanced features like message sessions and dead-lettering.

options.label: Basic — for testing and low traffic

name: Event Notification Hub
description: Publish events — like 'member record updated' or 'claim approved' — and let multiple systems react independently without needing to call each one individually.

steps.title: Topic Details
steps.description: Give your event hub a name and choose where it will be created.

fields.name: topicName
fields.label: What should we call this event hub?
fields.helpText: Use a name that describes the events it will carry, e.g. 'member-updates' or 'claims-events'.

fields.name: region
fields.label: Where should it be created?
fields.helpText: Choose a location close to the systems that will publish and receive events.

steps.title: Event Settings
steps.description: Choose the format that events will use when they are published to this hub.

fields.name: inputSchema
fields.label: What format should events use?
fields.helpText: Event Grid Schema is simpler to start with. Cloud Events 1.0 is an open standard — better if you need to connect to non-Microsoft platforms.

options.label: Event Grid Schema — simpler, recommended for getting started
Cloud Events 1.0 — open standard for cross-platform use

name: Full-Stack Web App
description: App Service + Azure SQL + Storage + Key Vault — a complete production-ready web stack.

steps.title: App Details
steps.description: Name your application and choose its compute tier.

fields.name: appName
fields.label: Application name
fields.helpText: Used as the base name for all resources in this stack.

fields.name: planSize
fields.label: App Service tier
fields.helpText: Choose the compute size for your web application.

steps.title: Database Settings
steps.description: Configure your Azure SQL database.

steps.title: Storage
steps.description: Configure blob storage for files and assets.

steps.title: Security
steps.description: Configure Key Vault for secrets management.

steps.title: Review
steps.description: Review your full-stack web app configuration before deploying.

name: Microservices Platform
description: Container Apps + Service Bus + Managed Identity — event-driven microservices infrastructure.

steps.title: App Details
steps.description: Name your platform and configure the container.

fields.name: appName
fields.label: Platform name
fields.helpText: Used as the base name for all resources.

fields.name: containerImage
fields.label: Container image
fields.helpText: Docker image to deploy into the Container App.

steps.title: Messaging
steps.description: Configure the Service Bus namespace for event-driven messaging.

steps.title: Identity
steps.description: Configure a Managed Identity for secure service-to-service communication.

fields.name: enableManagedIdentity
fields.label: Enable user-assigned managed identity?
fields.helpText: Recommended. Allows your Container App to access Azure services securely.

steps.title: Review
steps.description: Review your microservices platform configuration before deploying.

name: Data Pipeline
description: Storage Account + Azure Functions + Azure SQL — serverless data ingestion and processing.

steps.title: Pipeline Name
steps.description: Name your data pipeline.

fields.name: pipelineName
fields.label: Pipeline name
fields.helpText: Used as the base name for all resources.

steps.title: Storage
steps.description: Configure blob storage for raw pipeline data.

steps.title: Database
steps.description: Configure the Azure SQL destination database.

steps.title: Review
steps.description: Review your data pipeline configuration before deploying.

name: Secure API Backend
description: App Service + Key Vault + API Management + Log Analytics — a secure, observable API platform.

steps.title: API Details
steps.description: Name your API and choose the management tier.

fields.name: apiName
fields.label: API name
fields.helpText: Used as the base name for all resources.

fields.name: apimSku
fields.label: API Management tier
fields.helpText: Developer tier is suitable for non-production workloads.

steps.title: Security
steps.description: Configure Key Vault for secrets and certificate management.

steps.title: Observability
steps.description: Configure Log Analytics for monitoring and diagnostics.

fields.name: logRetentionDays
fields.label: Log retention (days)
fields.helpText: How long to retain logs in the workspace.

steps.title: Review
steps.description: Review your secure API backend configuration before deploying.
